research:Eval bar mechanics \u2014 exact.summary:
Lichess (lila) is the only major site with public eval-bar code, and it is fully extracted here. The pipeline is: (1) UCI parse layer converts side-to-move scores to white perspective; (2) a sigmoid ("winning chances") maps cp to [-1,1]; (3) the bar renders a single black div whose height% = 100 - (chances+1)*50, smoothed purely by CSS `transition: height 1s`.

The sigmoid (ui/lib/src/ceval/winningChances.ts): chances = 2/(1+exp(-0.00368208*cp)) - 1, with cp clamped to [-1000, 1000]. The constant was fit (PR #11148, scipy curve_fit) to 75k positions from 2300+ rapid games in the June 2022 lichess database, replacing the older -0.004. Lichess's published Win% (accuracy page) is the same function scaled: Win% = 50 + 50*(2/(1+exp(-0.00368208*cp))-1). Mate scores are mapped to a synthetic cp = (21 - min(10,|mate|))*100, signed — so M1→2000cp (chances 0.9987, bar 99.94% full), M10+→1100cp (0.9657, 98.3%). Because cp is clamped at 1000 (chances 0.9509, bar max 97.55% for non-mate), any forced mate always fills more than any cp eval, yet the bar is never pinned at 100% and still shrinks slightly as mate distance grows; the numeric label shows "#N". Game-over (mate 0) is filtered at parse time (`if (isMate && !povEv) return`).

Perspective: per the UCI spec, "cp [is] the score from the engine's point of view" (side to move at search root) and "mate in y moves, not plies; if the engine is getting mated use negative values". Lila converts to white-POV in protocol.ts: `const pivot = this.work.threatMode ? 0 : 1; const ev = this.work.ply % 2 === pivot ? -povEv : povEv;` (odd ply = black to move = negate; threat mode flips the pivot because a null move was injected). Board flipping never touches the math: renderGauge always computes povChances('white', ev) and adds class `reverse` when orientation is black, which CSS flips with `transform: rotateX(180deg)`. When no eval is available the gauge keeps the last value (`gaugeLast`) and dims via `.empty { opacity: 0.5 }`. Mid-search, every streamed `info` line re-renders the gauge and the 1s CSS transition absorbs depth-to-depth jumps.

Stockfish's native WDL: `win_rate_model(v,pos) = int(0.5 + 1000/(1+exp((a - v)/b)))` where a,b are cubic polynomials in m = clamp(material,17,78)/58 (material = pawns+3N+3B+5R+9Q); the `wdl` info field is "w d l" per-mille from the side-to-move perspective, with d = 1000-w-l. Enabled via `setoption name UCI_ShowWDL value true`; confirmed present in nmrugg/stockfish.js WASM source, so StockThink can use it client-side. Crucial caveats: it models engine self-play at fishtest LTC (60+0.6s) — far sharper than human outcomes (SF ≥15.1 normalizes cp so +100cp = 50% self-play win) — and it depends on material, so identical cp gives different fills across positions (bar would jump on captures; lichess issue #11026 debated and avoided this). Chess.com's bar formula is not public: their help center confirms only white-positive pawn units and ±M# notation. The open-source chess.com clone freechess uses a simpler linear clamp (blackHeight = clamp(H/2 - cp/3, 0, H), full bar + "M{n}" on mate, "1-0"/"0-1" on mate 0).

key_facts.claim: Lichess winning chances formula (current master, ui/lib/src/ceval/winningChances.ts): chances = 2/(1+Math.exp(-0.00368208*cp)) - 1, with cp clamped to [-1000,1000] before applying; result in [-1,1] from white's POV via toPov sign flip
Lichess published Win% = 50 + 50 * (2 / (1 + exp(-0.00368208 * centipawns)) - 1); identical sigmoid scaled to 0-100
The -0.00368208 multiplier was fit via scipy.optimize.curve_fit to 75,000 positions from 2300+ rated rapid games (June 2022 lichess database), replacing old -0.004 (lila PR #11148)

Lichess mate-to-bar mapping: synthetic cp = (21 - Math.min(10, Math.abs(mate))) * 100, signed by mate sign, then fed through the unclamped sigmoid. M1 = 2000cp-equiv (bar 99.94% full); M10+ = 1100cp-equiv (98.3%); max non-mate fill is 97.55% (cp clamp 1000) — so mate always out-fills any cp eval but is never pinned at 100%

Lichess gauge render: black div height = `100 - (ev + 1) * 50` % where ev = povChances('white', bestEv); keeps gaugeLast when eval missing (class 'empty'); adds class 'reverse' when orientation === 'black'

Lichess gauge smoothing is pure CSS: `.black { transition: height 1s; }`; orientation flip is pure CSS: `.reverse { transform: rotateX(180deg); }`; empty state `.empty { opacity: 0.5; }`; file ui/lib/css/ceval/_eval-gauge.scss

UCI spec: 'cp = the score from the engine's point of view in centipawns'; 'mate = mate in y moves, not plies. If the engine is getting mated use negative values for y'. Scores are from the side to move at the search-root position

Lila white-POV conversion (ui/lib/src/ceval/protocol.ts lines 119-120): `const pivot = this.work.threatMode ? 0 : 1; const ev = this.work.ply % 2 === pivot ? -povEv : povEv;` — odd ply = black to move = negate; threatMode flips pivot due to injected null move. Mate-0 lines are dropped: `if (isMate && !povEv) return;`

Stockfish win_rate_model (src/uci.cpp): returns int(0.5 + 1000/(1+std::exp((a - double(v))/b))) per-mille; a,b are degree-3 polynomials in m = clamp(material,17,78)/58.0, material = P + 3N + 3B + 5R + 9Q; coefficients as[]={-72.32565836,185.93832038,-144.58862193,416.44950446}, bs[]={83.86794042,-136.06112997,69.98820887,47.62901433} (current master; coefficients are refit per release from fishtest data)

Stockfish UCI 'wdl' info field = 'w d l' per-mille from side-to-move POV: wdl_w = win_rate_model(v), wdl_l = win_rate_model(-v), wdl_d = 1000 - w - l; enabled with setoption name UCI_ShowWDL value true

UCI_ShowWDL docs: 'WDL numbers model expected game outcomes for a given evaluation and game ply for engine self-play at fishtest LTC conditions (60+0.6s per game)' — i.e., self-play, not human, win rates

Since SF 15.1, reported cp is normalized: UCIEngine::to_cp(v,pos) = round(100 * v / a) so '100 centipawns' = 50% self-play win probability at fishtest LTC; this means SF's own cp scale is already win-prob anchored and differs from classical pawn-unit evals the lichess constant was fit against
UCI_ShowWDL and win_rate_model are present in nmrugg/stockfish.js (the standard browser WASM build) src/uci.cpp — native WDL is available client-side with no server

Freechess (open-source chess.com Game Review clone) eval bar uses a LINEAR clamp, not a sigmoid: blackHeight = Math.max(Math.min(totalHeight/2 - evaluation.value/3, totalHeight), 0) with totalHeight = 730px (saturates at ±1095cp); on mate: full bar via setEvalBarToWinner + label 'M'+|mate|; on mate 0: label '1-0' or '0-1'

Chess.com's exact bar-fill formula is not published; their help center confirms only: positive = White advantage in pawn units, forced mate shown as +M#/-M# with the bar fully one color. Clones reproduce the look with either the lichess sigmoid or a linear clamp

Lichess deliberately does NOT use engine WDL for the eval gauge (debated in issue #11026); WDL's material/ply dependence means the same cp fills the bar differently across positions and jumps on captures

Lichess mid-search behavior: renderGauge re-runs on every parsed streaming 'info' line (each depth/multipv update) and the 1s CSS height transition absorbs the jumps; no JS-side interpolation or easing exists

research:Eval bar mechanics \u2014 exact.recommendation: Adopt the lichess approach wholesale — it is proven, public, and trivially portable. Concretely: (1) Normalize signs once, at the UCI parse layer: Stockfish reports cp/mate from the side-to-move's perspective, so whiteEval = (stm === 'w') ? ev : -ev; store everything white-POV from then on (if you ever add a 'show threat' null-move search, flip the pivot like lila does). (2) Bar fill: port winningChances.ts verbatim — chances = 2/(1+exp(-0.00368208*clamp(cp,-1000,1000)))-1; mate via cpEquiv = sign(mate)*(21-min(10,|mate|))*100 through the unclamped sigmoid; blackDivHeight% = 100-(chances+1)*50. This gives chess.com-like behavior for free: forced mate renders a nearly-full bar (98.3–99.9%) that always exceeds any cp eval; label the bar 'M5'/'#5' for mate, '+2.3' otherwise, and '1-0'/'0-1' on actual game end (drop UCI 'mate 0'). Reuse the same povChances/povDiff for move classification deltas — lichess uses one function for gauge, graph, and accuracy, and StockThink should too. (3) Smoothing: do zero JS interpolation; set CSS transition: height 0.6–1s on the fill div and just re-render on each streamed 'info' line (throttle DOM writes to ~100-250ms); keep the last value with a dimmed 'empty' style when no eval is available yet. (4) Orientation: never touch the math — apply transform: rotateX(180deg) (or scaleY(-1)) to the gauge container when black is at the bottom. (5) WDL: enable UCI_ShowWDL (confirmed available in stockfish.js WASM builds) and parse 'wdl w d l' (per-mille, side-to-move POV — flip for white POV) to power an optional 'Win/Draw/Loss' readout per position, but do NOT drive the bar or accuracy from it: it models engine self-play at LTC (much sharper than human outcomes; SF≥15.1 already defines 100cp = 50% self-play win) and is material-dependent, so the bar would jump on captures — the reason lichess rejected it (issue #11026). One pitfall to document: because SF≥16 cp values are win-prob-normalized, they run slightly hotter than classical pawn units; the lichess constant still works well in practice since lichess itself runs these engines, so do not refit.

research:Frontend stack, PGN handling, a.summary:
Board UI: lichess chessground (npm `@lichess-org/chessground`) is the strongest fit: framework-agnostic, zero dependencies, 10K gzipped, GPL-3.0-or-later. Its DrawShape API natively supports everything StockThink needs: `autoShapes` for engine arrows, `customSvg` (100x100 viewBox HTML centered on orig/dest/label) for chess.com-style classification badges on squares, `label` text, custom brushes, and `highlight.custom` square classes. GPL is acceptable because Stockfish itself is GPL, making the whole app GPL anyway. Alternatives: cm-chessboard (MIT code, SVG, Markers/Arrows extensions, but bundled Staunty pieces are CC BY-NC-SA), chessboard2 (ISC, beta v0.5.0, stale since Feb 2024, no arrows), react-chessboard (customArrows/customSquareStyles props if React is chosen). Note chessground ships no CSS/pieces — copy base CSS and a piece set (e.g., cburnett, CC BY-SA) from lichess examples.

Game logic/PGN: chessops (GPL-3.0+, by niklasf/lichess) is the robust choice for pasted chess.com PGNs: its pgn module builds a full game tree (variations preserved), parses comments into {text, shapes, clock, emt, evaluation} — i.e., [%clk]/[%emt]/[%eval]/[%csl]/[%cal] — and `startingPosition(game.headers)` + `transform` cleanly yields a FEN per node. chess.js v1.4.0 works (permissive parser, comments retrievable via getComments(), history({verbose:true}) gives before/after FENs) but discards RAV variations and is not round-trippable; fine as a lightweight move-validation layer for the play-vs-engine board, weaker for review parsing.

Chess.com Game Review UX: summary screen shows per-player accuracy (typically 50–95, 100 possible), estimated game rating, classification-count table with icons for both players, phase icons (opening/middlegame/endgame), eval graph, coach bubble. Per-move: destination square tinted in the classification color, badge icon at the square's top-right corner, coach comment explaining WHY, "Show Line" plays best continuation, retry-mistakes recomputes adjusted accuracy. Expected-points-lost thresholds from the official support article: Best 0.00, Excellent ≤0.02, Good ≤0.05, Inaccuracy ≤0.10, Mistake ≤0.20, Blunder >0.20; Brilliant = sound piece sacrifice + (nearly) best + not already completely winning; Great = swings game outcome; Miss = failure to punish opponent's mistake. Replication colors (sampled by freechess/WintrChess): brilliant #1baaa6, great #5b8baf, best/excellent #98bc49, good/forced #97af8b, inaccuracy #f4bf44, mistake #e28c28, blunder #c93230, book #a88764. Badge geometry in freechess: icon at toX + (68/90)*sq, toY − (10/90)*sq, 56px on 160px squares; move squares tinted at 0.7 alpha.

Eval graph: lichess plots win-chance (not raw cp) as a chart.js line chart with white fill above origin / black below, orange line, classification-colored hover points. freechess hand-rolls a 350x80 canvas bar graph clamped at ±1100cp. A hand-rolled SVG area chart is recommended (no dependency).

Deployment: GitHub Pages serves .wasm as `application/wasm` with `access-control-allow-origin: *` (verified by curl) so instantiateStreaming works; but it cannot set COOP/COEP, so SharedArrayBuffer (multithreaded Stockfish) needs coi-serviceworker (MIT, must be an unbundled same-origin file next to index.html; SW scope on a project page is /stockthink/, which works). Vite needs `base: '/<REPO>/'`; the official actions/deploy-pages workflow is captured verbatim.

Play-vs-engine levels (verbatim from lichess fishnet): levels 1–8 map to Skill Level −9/−5/−1/3/7/11/16/20, depth 5/5/5/5/5/8/13/22, movetime 50/100/150/200/300/400/500/1000ms. Stockfish UCI_LimitStrength+UCI_Elo covers 1320–3190 and overrides Skill Level; below 1320 use Skill Level + shallow depth.

key_facts.claim: Chessground is framework-agnostic, zero-dependency, 10K gzipped (31K unzipped), GPL-3.0-or-later, installed as npm `@lichess-org/chessground`; using it requires distributing the combined work under GPL

Chessground DrawShape supports classification badges natively: `customSvg: { html: string; center?: 'orig'|'dest'|'label' }` (midpoint of 100x100 viewbox), `label: { text, fill }` ('label moved to top right of square' when no brush), plus `drawable.autoShapes` for computer/engine arrows and `highlight.custom` (SquareClasses) for custom square highlights
Chessground ships no CSS or piece images; base CSS and piece sets must be copied from the lichess examples repo / lila assets

cm-chessboard: MIT-licensed code, SVG rendering, ES6, no dependencies, with Markers, Arrows, Accessibility, PromotionDialog and RightClickAnnotator extensions; BUT bundled Staunty piece SVGs are CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (non-commercial) and Wikimedia pieces CC BY-SA 3.0
chessboard2 (@chrisoakman/chessboard2) is ISC-licensed, v0.5.0, last pushed 2024-02-17 — beta and effectively stale; no built-in arrow/badge system comparable to chessground

react-chessboard supports customArrows as tuples [['a3','a5','red'],['g1','f3']], customSquareStyles/customDarkSquareStyle inline CSS objects, drag-drop, and animated programmatic position changes — best option only if React is chosen

chess.js v1.4.0 loadPgn DISCARDS recursive annotation variations (RAV); comments are preserved and retrievable via getComments() returning [{fen, comment}]; default permissive parser accepts non-standard SAN; history({verbose:true}) returns before/after FENs per move — adequate for mainline-only analysis but lossy for annotated PGNs

chessops (GPL-3.0-or-later) pgn module preserves full game trees including variations, and parseComment returns {text, shapes, clock, emt, evaluation} — directly parsing chess.com [%clk], plus [%emt], [%eval], [%csl], [%cal]; the parser 'will interpret any input as a PGN... skipping any invalid tokens' (DoS-resistant streaming parser included); startingPosition(game.headers) honors FEN/SetUp headers

Chess.com expected-points-lost classification thresholds: Best 0.00, Excellent 0.00–0.02, Good 0.02–0.05, Inaccuracy 0.05–0.10, Mistake 0.10–0.20, Blunder 0.20–1.00; Brilliant = good piece sacrifice + best-or-nearly-best + not already completely winning (rating-adjusted); Great = move critical to game outcome (losing→equal or equal→winning); Miss = failing to capitalize on opponent's mistake

Replica classification colors used by freechess AND wintrchess (sampled from chess.com): brilliant #1baaa6, great/critical #5b8baf, best #98bc49, excellent #98bc49, good/okay #97af8b, inaccuracy #f4bf44, mistake #e28c28, blunder #c93230, forced #97af8b, book/theory #a88764, risky #8983ac; ready-made badge PNG icons exist in freechess src/public/media/ (brilliant/great/best/excellent/good/book/inaccuracy/mistake/blunder/forced .png) and wintrchess client/public/img/classifications/ (adds miss.png, critical.png, risky.png)

freechess badge placement formula: classification icon drawn at destination-square top-right corner — x = toX*sq + (68/90)*sq, y = toY*sq − (10/90)*sq, icon 56px where sq = 160px (1280px board); both last-move squares are filled with the classification color at globalAlpha 0.7; best-move suggestion arrow drawn in classificationColours.best (#98bc49) width 35 opacity 0.8 (2nd line: width 21, opacity 0.55)

Chess.com Game Review UX: per-player accuracy typically 50–95 (100 possible), estimated game rating, classification-count table for both players, phase icons for opening/middlegame/endgame, coach bubble explaining WHY a move is good/bad, 'Show Line' plays out the engine's best continuation on the board, retry-mistakes yields an adjusted accuracy score, opening names + master commentary; eval graph has gray centerline (equality) with colored dots per classification (brown=book, red=blunder, green=best, orange=mistake, blue=great, pink=miss, dull green=good, yellow=inaccuracy); eval bar beside board; top-3 engine lines in white boxes

Lichess advantage chart (ui/chart/src/acpl.ts) uses chart.js 4.x: plots WIN-CHANCE (winningChances.povChances('white',{cp})) not raw centipawns ('Plot winchance because logarithmic'), line with fill target:'origin' (white above / black below), orange accent border, classification-colored hover points; mate = ±Infinity cp; freechess instead hand-rolls a 350x80 canvas bar chart clamped at ±1100cp with classification-colored overlays on hover/current move

GitHub Pages serves .wasm files with 'content-type: application/wasm' and 'access-control-allow-origin: *' (verified live via curl -I https://mdn.github.io/webassembly-examples/js-api-examples/simple.wasm), so WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming works without fallback

GitHub Pages cannot set COOP/COEP headers, so SharedArrayBuffer (needed only by MULTI-threaded Stockfish builds) requires coi-serviceworker: MIT, must be a separate un-bundled file served from your own origin adjacent to index.html (or a parent dir), included via <script src="coi-serviceworker.js"></script>; it reloads the page once; on a project page the SW scope is /stockthink/ which fully covers the app. Single-threaded Stockfish builds need none of this

Vite project-page deployment: set base: '/<REPO>/' in vite.config (e.g. '/stockthink/'); official GitHub Actions workflow uses actions/checkout → setup-node → npm ci → npm run build → actions/configure-pages → actions/upload-pages-artifact (path './dist') → actions/deploy-pages, with permissions contents:read, pages:write, id-token:write

Lichess play-vs-computer level mapping (verbatim from fishnet src/api.rs): Skill Level per level 1-8 = [-9,-5,-1,3,7,11,16,20]; depth = [5,5,5,5,5,8,13,22]; movetime ms = [50,100,150,200,300,400,500,1000]; lichess uses Fairy-Stockfish to allow negative skill levels — plain Stockfish clamps Skill Level to 0-20, so levels 1-3 must be approximated (e.g. Skill 0 + depth 1-3 or random-blunder injection)
Stockfish UCI_LimitStrength (default false) + UCI_Elo (1320–3190, calibrated at 120s+1s vs CCRL 40/4) overrides Skill Level; below 1320 Elo, combine Skill Level 0–20 with shallow depth limits

WintrChess (freechess successor, github.com/WintrCat/wintrchess) renamed classifications: great→critical, good→okay, book→theory, added 'risky' (#8983ac, NAG $5) and per-classification NAG export ($3 brilliant, $1 critical, $2 mistake, $4 blunder, $6 inaccuracy); freechess itself is abandoned but its full Game-Review-clone UI (eval bar, eval graph, badges, accuracy) is readable reference code

research:Frontend stack, PGN handling, a.recommendation: Stack: Vite + vanilla TypeScript (smallest payload, no framework tax) with chessground (@lichess-org/chessground) as the board — it is the only library with first-class autoShapes/customSvg/label support, which directly implements classification badges on squares, best-move arrows, and last-move highlights without hacks; GPL-3.0 is a non-issue since Stockfish makes the app GPL regardless. Copy chessground base CSS + a cburnett piece set from lichess examples (avoid cm-chessboard's CC BY-NC-SA Staunty pieces). Use chessops for PGN ingestion (handles chess.com exports' %clk comments, NAGs, variations, FEN headers losslessly; use the documented transform pattern to attach a FEN to every mainline node for the engine queue) and optionally chess.js only for the simple play-vs-engine move validation if its API feels lighter. Replicate the chess.com report with: summary card (per-player accuracy, estimated rating, classification-count table using the freechess/wintrchess palette and badge icon style — draw badges as inline SVG circles with !!/!/star glyphs at the destination square's top-right corner via chessground customSvg at center:'label'), per-move coach comment under the move list with an expandable elaboration, and Show-Line that plays the PV on the board. Hand-roll the eval graph as an SVG area chart (no chart dependency): plot win-percentage (lichess povChances) not raw cp, white-above/black-below fill, gray midline, classification-colored dots, click-to-jump — freechess's canvas version is the reference. Deploy with the official Vite Pages workflow (base '/stockthink/', actions/upload-pages-artifact + deploy-pages); prefer a single-threaded Stockfish NNUE build to avoid SharedArrayBuffer entirely (Pages serves wasm MIME correctly, verified), adding coi-serviceworker only if multithreading is later wanted. For play-vs-engine levels, expose 8 levels using the fishnet mapping (clamp Skill Level to 0 for levels 1–3 with reduced depth 1–3, or use UCI_LimitStrength/UCI_Elo 1320–3190 for calibrated strengths), then feed the finished game's move list straight into the same review pipeline.

research:Survey ALL open-source prior ar.summary: Surveyed all notable open-source chess.com-style game review prior art, reading actual source code. (1) wintrcat/freechess (842 stars, ABANDONED, license CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 — NonCommercial/ShareAlike, NOT reusable in a permissively-licensed project; ideas/algorithms are fine, verbatim code is not). Its Express server is trivial (PGN parse + classification + reCAPTCHA); all Stockfish evaluation already runs in the BROWSER via up to 8 single-threaded stockfish.js web workers plus free no-key Lichess cloud-eval (multiPv=2) for opening positions — proving a 100% static GitHub Pages version is feasible. Classification uses quadratic centipawn-loss thresholds as functions of |prevEval|, special mate-transition tables, brilliant = winning-side sacrifice of a hanging piece that can't be viably captured, great = punishing a blunder when top-2 engine moves differ ≥150cp; accuracy = naive mean of per-class point values (blunder 0…best 1)×100 — NOT chess.com's method. No natural-language commentary at all (only badges, opening names, engine lines). Known issues: brilliant false positives (#13), F5 breaks page (#38), UI overlaps. (2) Successor wintrcat/wintrchess (205 stars, GPL-3.0) is the most refined clone: expected-points (logistic 1/(1+e^(-0.0035cp))) loss thresholds Best<0.01/Excellent<0.045/Okay<0.08/Inaccuracy<0.12/Mistake<0.22/else Blunder; adds CRITICAL (only-good-move: 2nd-best line loses ≥0.1 EP) and a piece-safety/trapped-piece-based brilliant detector; move accuracy = 103.16·e^(−4·EPloss)−3.17. (3) GuillaumeSD/Chesskit (380 stars, AGPL-3.0, Next.js): analysis fully client-side with bundled single-threaded Stockfish 11–18 WASM; uses Lichess win% formula (50+50·(2/(1+e^(−0.00368208·cp))−1), cp clamped ±1000, mate→100/0); win%-diff classes Blunder<−20/Mistake<−10/Inaccuracy<−5/Okay<−2/else Excellent; Splendid(brilliant)=sacrifice+≤2% drop+not losing+alternative not >97%; Perfect(great)=outcome flip across 50% or only-good-move (+10%), excluding simple recaptures; accuracy is a faithful port of Lichess lila (weighted+harmonic mean) plus ACPL→Elo estimate 3100·e^(−0.01·ACPL). (4) Lichess lila (AGPL-3.0): accuracy 103.1668·e^(−0.043544·winDiff)−3.1669+1; game accuracy = mean(volatility-weighted mean, harmonic mean), window=(moves/10) clamped 2..8, weight=stddev of win% clamped 0.5..12; Inaccuracy/Mistake/Blunder at winning-chances delta 0.1/0.2/0.3 (−1..1 scale); template commentary is literally "Mistake. Nf3 was best."; Learn From Your Mistakes = evalSwings (povDiff>0.1 or lost mate≤3) → "Find a better move for White" → fail "You can do better" / "Best was X" / skip / completion counter, master-book moves excluded; client mate→cp mapping (21−min(10,|mate|))·100. (5) en-croissant (1652 stars, GPL-3.0, Tauri desktop, not browser): ?!/?/?? at >5/>10/>20 win% loss; "!"=best move that punishes (top-2 gap>10% and prev move worsened >5%); "!!"=same+sacrifice (naive material alpha-beta in Rust); "!?"=sacrifice, not only-move, nextCP>−200. (6) Non-LLM commentary: LinkAnJarad/OpenChess-Insights (71 stars, MIT!) has the best template/rule-based commentary engine: ~40 pure-board-logic detectors (hanging/defended pieces, forks created/allowed/missed, pins/missed pins, discovered check+attack, trapped pieces, tempo, fianchetto, development, rook-to-open-file, free/higher captures, sacrifice→brilliant, mate threats, "The opponent can play X") composing sentences like "This move leaves Queen hanging on f7. The opponent can play Kxf7." — but it's Python/Flask, 5–10 min per game, not static. python-chess-annotator (62 stars, GPL-3.0, archived) only emits NAGs+PV+eval, minimal prose. Long tail (chessbro CC BY-NC, React-Chess-Analyze MIT, Razamindset clones, stockfish-chess-web-gui AGPL) adds nothing new. Nobody implements chess.com's "Miss" or per-move explain-more prose — both are open differentiators for StockThink.

key_facts.claim: freechess is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (NonCommercial, ShareAlike) — verbatim code reuse is incompatible with MIT/GPL hosting of StockThink; reimplement algorithms instead (LICENSE file, 20,849 bytes, title 'Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International Public License')
freechess is abandoned (readme says so), 842 stars, 213 forks; successor is wintrchess

freechess architecture: Express server only does POST /api/parse (chess.js PGN->FENs) and POST /api/report (pure-TS analyse() + reCAPTCHA). ALL Stockfish evaluation runs client-side: lichess cloud-eval API (https://lichess.org/api/cloud-eval?fen=...&multiPv=2, free, no key) first, then up to 8 parallel single-threaded Stockfish web workers at user-chosen depth. Therefore the whole pipeline can run fully static on GitHub Pages

freechess classifications: brilliant/great/best/excellent/good/inaccuracy/mistake/blunder/book/forced (NO 'miss'); accuracy = mean of per-move classification values (blunder 0, mistake 0.2, inaccuracy 0.4, good 0.65, excellent 0.9, rest 1.0) x100 — much cruder than chess.com/lichess win%-based accuracy

freechess centipawn classification uses quadratic eval-loss thresholds in |prevEval| (self-described '// WTF Algorithm'), e.g. MISTAKE threshold = 0.0003*prevEval^2 + 0.4027*prevEval + 225.8182 centipawns; plus hard-coded mate-transition tables; blunder is downgraded to 'good' if position still >= +600cp or was already <= -600cp

freechess GREAT move rule: previous move classified blunder AND |top1−top2| >= 150cp AND moved piece not hanging; BRILLIANT rule: eval >= 0 for mover, not winning anyway (2nd line < +700cp), non-promotion, leaves a non-pawn piece sacked/hanging whose every capture loses material/is pinned (rook+ sacrifices ignore mate-in-1 refutation, minor pieces don't)

freechess has NO natural-language commentary — report shows badges, accuracies, opening name, engine line SANs only; known open issues: brilliant false positives (#13), site breaks on refresh (#38), overlapping UI (#53, #34), reCAPTCHA bug (#60)
wintrchess (successor, 205 stars) is GPL-3.0, monorepo client(React+TS)/server/shared; all classification logic is in shared/src/lib/reporter (pure TS + chess.js, portable to browser)

wintrchess classifies via expected-points loss (logistic 1/(1+exp(-0.0035*cp)); mate=>0/1): Best<0.01, Excellent<0.045, Okay<0.08, Inaccuracy<0.12, Mistake<0.22, else Blunder; enum adds CRITICAL (= chess.com Great: top move played, not free capture, 2nd-best line loses >=0.1 EP), RISKY, THEORY, FORCED; move accuracy = 103.16*exp(-4*pointLoss) - 3.17

Chesskit (380 stars, AGPL-3.0, Next.js/React/MUI) runs game review fully client-side with bundled single-threaded Stockfish WASM builds (11, 16, 16.1, 17, 17.1, 18 incl. '-lite-single' ~6MB versions) in public/engines — the pattern proves multi-engine-version client analysis works without COOP/COEP headers

Chesskit classification (win% scale): Blunder < -20, Mistake < -10, Inaccuracy < -5, Okay < -2, else Excellent; Best if played == bestMove; Splendid (brilliant) = piece sacrifice + winDiff >= -2 + mover not losing + alternative line not >97% winning; Perfect (great) = winDiff >= -2 + (outcome flips across 50% with >10 swing OR only good move >10% better than alternative), excluding simple recaptures

Lichess win% formula (used by Chesskit, en-croissant, wintrchess variant): Win% = 50 + 50*(2/(1+exp(-0.00368208*cp)) - 1), cp clamped to ±1000; mate => 100 or 0 (Chesskit) or via cp=(21-min(10,|mate|))*100 (lila client)

Lichess accuracy: per-move = clamp(103.1668100711649*exp(-0.04354415386753951*winDiff) - 3.166924740191411 + 1, 0, 100); game accuracy = mean(volatility-weighted mean, harmonic mean) where window size = (#moves/10) clamped [2,8] and weight = stddev of window win% clamped [0.5,12]

Lichess judgments: Inaccuracy/Mistake/Blunder at winning-chances delta >= 0.1/0.2/0.3 (on -1..1 scale; client povDiff is halved); mate advice: MateCreated => Inaccuracy if prevCp<-999, Mistake if <-700, else Blunder; MateLost => Inaccuracy if cp>999, Mistake if >700, else Blunder; MateDelayed => no judgment. Generated comment template is exactly: '<Judgement>. <firstVariationMove> was best.' e.g. 'Mistake. Nf3 was best.'

Lichess 'Learn from your mistakes': candidate plies = evalSwings = own-color nodes where |povDiff| > 0.1 OR a mate <= 3 was lost; UX = 'Find a better move for White' -> on wrong try 'You can do better' -> 'View the solution' ('Best was X') / 'Skip this move' -> completion counter; moves found in master opening explorer (>1 game) are auto-excluded

en-croissant (1652 stars, GPL-3.0) is a Tauri DESKTOP app (Rust backend) — not GitHub-Pages compatible; annotation thresholds: ?! if win% loss >5, ? if >10, ?? if >20; '!' = played best move where top-2 gap >10 win% and it punishes (best move gained >5 win% vs two plies ago); '!!' = same conditions + is_sacrifice (detected by a lightweight material-only alpha-beta 'naive_eval' in Rust); '!?' = sacrifice that isn't only-move with nextCP > -200; requires MultiPV 2; accuracy = same lichess formula

OpenChess-Insights (71 stars, MIT — only permissively-licensed project in the field) contains a complete rule-based NL commentary engine: ~40 detector functions in chess_review.py (is_hanging, move_hangs_piece, move_creates_fork/allows_fork/misses_fork, move_pins_opponent/misses_pin, move_is_discovered_check_and_attacks, move_traps_opponents_piece, move_wins_tempo, is_developing_move, is_fianchetto, moves_rook_to_open_file, move_moves_king_off_backrank, move_captures_free_piece/higher_piece, is_possible_sacrifice, move_threatens_mate, is_possible_trade, ...) composed into template sentences; MIT license allows porting these detectors to TypeScript/chess.js

OpenChess-Insights sentence quality examples (verbatim templates): 'This is a book move. The opening is called {opening}.', '{san} is {classification}.', 'This move leaves {pieces} hanging on {squares}.', 'This creates a fork on {pieces}.', 'There was a missed fork with {san}.', 'This creates a discovered check whilst attacking a {pieces}.', 'This sacrifices the {piece}.', 'This creates a checkmate threat.', 'The opponent can play {san}.' — readable but mechanical; grammar bugs exist ('looses', 'is mistake'); classification uses raw cp loss (excellent>=-20, good>=-100, inaccuracy>=-250, mistake>=-450, else blunder); NOT static-hostable as-is (Python/Flask + local Stockfish, 5-10 min per 30-move game)

python-chess-annotator (rpdelaney, 62 stars, GPL-3.0, archived 'rpdelaney-archive'): adds NAG ??/?/?! at raw eval delta < -300/-150/-75 cp; only annotates when win% delta > 7.5 using winning_chances = 50+50*(2/(1+e^(-0.004*cp))-1); comments are just PV + numeric eval ('0.90/25', 'Mate in 4') + game-end reasons + ACPL header — minimal prose, poor quality bar to beat
Chesskit Elo estimate: eloFromCpl = 3100*exp(-0.01*averageCpl), blended with known rating via rating*exp(-0.005*cplDiff)

Long-tail clones add little: chessbro-org/chessbro (14 stars, CC BY-NC 4.0 modified, Vite/React client-side, basic ratings only); HaikalE/React-Chess-Analyze (0 stars, MIT claimed, client-only React 19 + web workers, freechess-style); Razamindset/chess-com-game-review and knightly (Next.js, tiny); LabinatorSolutions/stockfish-chess-web-gui (70 stars, AGPL-3.0, plain-JS play/analysis GUI, static-hostable but no game review report); goodvibs/eval.bar (8 stars, NO license); spencerjireh/aftermove (MIT, desktop). NONE generate chess.com-grade per-move commentary

No surveyed open-source project implements chess.com's 'Miss' classification (wintrchess ships a miss.png asset but it is absent from its Classification enum), and none offers an 'explain more' elaboration layer — both are open differentiators for StockThink

GitHub Pages constraint pattern: GitHub Pages cannot set COOP/COEP headers, so multi-threaded (SharedArrayBuffer) Stockfish is unavailable; both freechess (8 parallel single-threaded workers) and Chesskit ('-single'/'lite-single' builds) solve this with single-threaded WASM engines, optionally parallelized across multiple Worker instances

research:Survey ALL open-source prior ar.recommendation: 1) Architecture: go fully static — prior art proves it. freechess already evaluates entirely in-browser (its server only parses PGN and runs a pure chess.js analyse(); both trivially run client-side), and Chesskit ships Stockfish WASM in public/ with all classification/accuracy in client TS. Use single-threaded Stockfish WASM builds (e.g. 'lite-single' NNUE, ~6MB) because GitHub Pages cannot serve COOP/COEP headers for SharedArrayBuffer multithreading; parallelize like freechess with N (4-8) single-threaded Workers each owning one FEN, with MultiPV=2 (required for Great/Brilliant/only-move logic). Optionally pre-fill opening plies from the free, key-less Lichess cloud-eval API (multiPv=2) and tag them Book, with graceful fallback on 404/429. 2) Licensing: do NOT copy freechess code (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, incompatible and non-commercial); its thresholds/algorithms may be reimplemented as uncopyrightable methods but prefer not to — its quadratic-cp 'WTF Algorithm' and naive accuracy are the weakest of the surveyed designs anyway. Base classification on the Lichess win% model (Win% = 50+50*(2/(1+e^(-0.00368208cp))-1), accuracy 103.1668·e^(−0.043544·winDiff)−3.1669+1, weighted+harmonic game accuracy) — formulas are facts and already re-implemented in MIT-adjacent ecosystems. If you want to port code verbatim, the cleanest sources are Chesskit (AGPL-3.0) and wintrchess (GPL-3.0), which would force StockThink to be (A)GPL — acceptable for a free GitHub Pages project, but if you want MIT, clean-room the classifier (it's <300 lines) and port concept detectors from OpenChess-Insights, the ONLY MIT project, translating its ~40 python-chess detectors to chess.js. 3) Classification design: use Chesskit/wintrchess-style win%/expected-points thresholds (Blunder/Mistake/Inaccuracy/Okay at −20/−10/−5/−2 win%, or wintrchess EP cutoffs), Best = top engine move, Great = only-good-move or outcome-flip (exclude simple recaptures, exclude free captures), Brilliant = sound sacrifice while not losing and not already crushing, Forced when one legal move, Book from an openings FEN table; add freechess's anti-noise rule (no Blunder when already ±600cp) and lila's mate-transition tables. Implement chess.com's 'Miss' (no open project has it): opponent's last move was Mistake/Blunder and you failed to play the punishing move that the engine shows wins >=10 win% over your played move, or you dropped a mate-in-N to eval. 4) Commentary: nobody does this well — lichess emits only 'Mistake. Nf3 was best.'; freechess/wintrchess/Chesskit emit nothing. Differentiate with a two-layer template engine: short line = classification + dominant detected motif ('Bxh7 is a mistake — it leaves your knight on d4 hanging.'); 'explain more' = full fact list (motifs from the OpenChess-Insights detector inventory: hanging/fork/pin/discovered check/trap/tempo/development/mate threat/missed tactics) + best-line PV walkthrough + opponent's best reply ('The opponent can play ...'). Fix OpenChess-Insights' grammar bugs and gate each sentence on the classification tier. 5) Also port: lichess 'Learn from your mistakes' flow (evalSwings povDiff>0.1 candidates, find-better-move loop with 'You can do better' / 'Best was X' / skip / progress) — high-value, low-cost, absent from every clone; and Chesskit's ACPL→Elo estimate (3100·e^(−0.01·ACPL)) as a fun extra. 6) Avoid known failure modes: freechess brilliant false positives (use wintrchess's piece-safety/trapped-piece refinements), page-refresh state loss (#38), UI overlap of suggestion arrows (#53), and never hard-require a CAPTCHA/server.

research:Running Stockfish in the browse.summary: Browser Stockfish engine layer for a static GitHub Pages app is fully solved territory with two main build families. (1) nmrugg/stockfish.js (npm "stockfish", v18.0.7, GPLv3, copyright Chess.com 2026) ships 5 flavors of Stockfish 18; the key one for us is stockfish-18-lite-single (.js 20.7KB + .wasm 7.3MB, NNUE small net embedded in the wasm, single-threaded, needs NO SharedArrayBuffer/COOP/COEP) — the package README explicitly recommends it ("fast and does not require any complicated setup... still far stronger than any human"). The full-strength builds are 113MB wasm — over GitHub's 100MB per-file git limit and over jsDelivr's 50MB file limit, so effectively unusable for us. Usage is dead simple: new Worker('stockfish-18-lite-single.js'), postMessage UCI strings, onmessage receives output lines. (2) lichess's @lichess-org/stockfish-web (v0.4.0, successor of lila-stockfish-web@0.0.11) has tiny wasms (sf_18.wasm 700KB, sf_18_smallnet.wasm 665KB) that load .nnue files separately via setNnueBuffer(Uint8Array, index) — big net nn-c288c895ea92.nnue = 72,754,437 B, small nets nn-4ca89e4b3abf.nnue = 10,419,881 B / nn-37f18f62d772.nnue = 2,876,624 B — but ALL its builds require ['sharedMem','simd','dynamicImportFromWorker'] per lila's engines.ts, i.e. crossOriginIsolated is mandatory. hi-ogawa's repos are historical (his SIMD/NNUE work is merged into both families). SharedArrayBuffer on GitHub Pages: gzuidhof/coi-serviceworker (MIT) works — it intercepts fetches in a service worker and injects COEP/COOP headers; caveats: must be served same-origin (no CDN), HTTPS required, reloads the page on first visit (sessionStorage-guarded), defaults to COEP:credentialless then auto-degrades to require-corp via a second reload (Safari doesn't support credentialless, so Safari users get two reloads), silently fails in Firefox private mode (no serviceWorker). Since single-threaded lite needs none of this, coi-serviceworker should be an optional progressive enhancement for a multi-threaded "strong mode" (stockfish-18-lite.js, also 7MB). Analysis depth calibration from primary sources: lichess fishnet (server analysis) Work.scala uses nodesPerMove = 1,000,000 (manualRequest, NNUE) — 5,000,000 for broadcasts, only 100,000 for autoTutor (lichess's game-review-like product) — classical engines get nodes*3. Chess.com runs game review server-side on full-NNUE SF16/16.1 and says quality has "diminishing returns... past the low 20's" depth; their free in-browser analysis uses a lite engine. So review-grade client analysis ≈ "go nodes 100000-1000000" per position; with single-thread WASM small-net at roughly 200-700 knps desktop (estimate; WASM ≈ 2x slower than native per lichess testing), "go movetime 500" or "go nodes 250000" gives depth ~14-18 and analyzes 80 plies in 40-80s on one worker, half that with a 2-worker pool. lila's protocol.ts is a complete, battle-tested verbatim reference for the UCI layer: options to set (UCI_AnalyseMode=true, UCI_Chess960=true, Threads, Hash, MultiPV), 'position fen X moves ...', 'go <by> <value>', full info-line parser (depth/nodes/multipv/time/score cp|mate/lowerbound/pv), POV sign flip by ply parity, multipv array assembly, stop/swapWork queueing. Mobile gotchas from lila: maxHash 32MB iOS / 64MB iPad & Android vs 512MB desktop; sharedWasmMemory() shrink-retry loop for WebAssembly.Memory allocation failures; NNUE buffers cached in OPFS/IDB ("bigFileStorage") with corrupt-file (BAD_NNUE) eviction. GitHub Pages limits: 1GB site, 100GB/month soft bandwidth, 100MB/file git hard limit — 7MB lite wasm is trivially hostable in-repo; jsDelivr (npm/stockfish@18.0.7) works as fallback for files <50MB.

key_facts.claim: npm package 'stockfish' (nmrugg/stockfish.js) v18.0.7 = Stockfish 18, GPLv3, (c) 2026 Chess.com LLC; sponsored by Chess.com for their in-browser engine

stockfish@18.0.7 exact file sizes: bin/stockfish-18-lite-single.js 20,670 B + .wasm 7,295,411 B; stockfish-18-lite.js 32,109 B + .wasm 7,093,151 B (multithreaded); stockfish-18-single.wasm 112,992,459 B; stockfish-18.wasm 113,007,340 B; stockfish-18-asm.js 10,509,235 B

README recommendation: 'most likely, you should use the lite single-threaded engine because it is fast and does not require any complicated setup... the lite engine is still far stronger than any human will ever be'; multithreaded flavors 'will only run in browsers with the proper CORS headers applied' (COOP/COEP); single-threaded flavors run without them

SF18 has no HCE fallback (classical eval removed after SF16); the 7MB lite wasm must embed a small NNUE net (no .nnue files exist in the npm package); chess.com support: 'lite versions will use smaller NNUEs instead of handcrafted evaluation'

Browser usage of stockfish.js = plain Web Worker: new Worker(path) then postMessage of raw UCI strings, output lines via onmessage (loadEngine.js: 'if (typeof Worker === "function") { return new Worker(path); }')
WASM builds support Chrome/Edge/Firefox/Opera/Safari on Windows 10+/macOS 11+/iOS 16+/Linux/Android; asm.js fallback (10MB, 'very slow and weak, last resort') runs anywhere

@lichess-org/stockfish-web v0.4.0 contains sf_18.js (26KB) + sf_18.wasm (700,725 B), sf_18_smallnet.wasm (665,161 B), fsf_14.wasm (971,033 B), plus _relaxed-simd variants; NNUE nets are NOT bundled — loaded at runtime via setNnueBuffer(Uint8Array, index) / getRecommendedNnue(index); GPLv3

ALL lichess stockfish-web builds require SharedArrayBuffer: lila engines.ts declares requires: ['sharedMem','simd','dynamicImportFromWorker'] for sf_18, sf_18_smallnet, fsf_14 — they cannot run without crossOriginIsolated; lichess's no-SAB fallback is ancient SF11-era HCE (npm/stockfish.js 'sfwasm' SimpleEngine)

Exact NNUE net sizes (HTTP range probe on tests.stockfishchess.org): SF18 big nn-c288c895ea92.nnue = 72,754,437 B; SF17.1 big nn-1c0000000000.nnue = 61,698,882 B; sf_18_smallnet net nn-4ca89e4b3abf.nnue = 10,419,881 B; dual-net small nn-37f18f62d772.nnue = 2,876,624 B. Lichess UI labels: 'Stockfish 18 · 108MB' (minMem 2560) vs 'Stockfish 18 · 15MB' smallnet (minMem 1536)

lila-stockfish-web@0.0.11 (older pkg name) has sf171-79 = official SF 17.1 (big nn-1c0000000000 + small nn-37f18f62d772) and sf16-7 = linrock SF16 with single 7MB-class net nn-ecb35f70ff2a — useful if we want SF17.1 instead of 18

coi-serviceworker (gzuidhof, MIT, v0.1.7) makes GitHub Pages crossOriginIsolated by rewriting all responses in a service worker: sets COEP (credentialless or require-corp), COOP same-origin, CORP cross-origin; it 'will reload the page on the user's first load'; must be a separate same-origin file (no CDN, no bundling); requires HTTPS/localhost

coi-serviceworker pitfalls: defaults to coepCredentialless()=>true but Safari does not support COEP:credentialless, so the script's coepDegrade path triggers a SECOND reload to fall back to require-corp on Safari; fails silently where navigator.serviceWorker is unavailable (e.g. Firefox private mode); sessionStorage keys coiReloadedBySelf/coiCoepHasFailed guard against reload loops

Safari supports SharedArrayBuffer only when crossOriginIsolated (since Safari 15.2, WebKit bug 229559); generic service workers that strip/forget COOP+COEP headers silently kill crossOriginIsolated on cache hits — always assert window.crossOriginIsolated before choosing the threaded engine

Lichess server analysis node budgets (current master): Origin.manualRequest = 1,000,000 nodes/move (user-requested game/study analysis), officialBroadcast = 5,000,000, autoHunter = 300,000, autoTutor (lichess's game-review-like feature) = 100,000; classical engines get nodes*3 (JsonApi: sf18/sf17_1/sf16 -> a.nodes, classical -> a.nodes*3); fishnet hardware bar is '~2 meganodes in 6 seconds'

Chess.com Game Review runs server-side 'Stockfish 16 or 16.1 with the full NNUE'; they found 'diminishing returns in engine quality as depth gets past the low 20s'; free users' in-browser engine is a lite build; 'The full NNUE is 45 MB, which we cannot force-download onto every user's machine'

Single-thread browser WASM speed: lichess testing found WASM ~2-2.5x slower than native; realistic small-net NNUE single-thread ≈ 200-700 knps on desktop, ~50-200 knps mobile => 'go nodes 250000' ≈ 0.4-1.2s/position, depth ~14-18; 80 plies analyzable in ~40-80s on one worker, ~half with two pooled workers. (Estimates — benchmark with the engine's 'bench' command at runtime and adapt movetime.)

UCI essentials: 'go depth N' / 'go movetime ms' / 'go nodes N'; 'position fen <fen> moves <uci...>'; MultiPV spin default 1 max 500; info line format 'info depth 1 seldepth 1 multipv 1 score cp 18 nodes 20 nps 10000 hashfull 0 tbhits 0 time 2 pv e2e4'; 'bestmove e2e4 ponder e7e6'; score cp is from side-to-move POV (lila negates by ply parity); skip 'lowerbound'/'upperbound' info lines for multipv 1; Threads default 1 max 1024, Hash default 16MB

lila protocol.ts (complete reusable reference) sets: UCI_AnalyseMode=true, 'Analysis Contempt'=Off, UCI_Chess960=true, then ucinewgame + isready; queues one pending work item, sends 'stop' and swaps on bestmove; assembles multipv pvs[] and only emits when multiPv === expectedPvs

Mobile limits from lila: maxHashMB = 32 on iOS, 64 on iPad ('iPadOS safari pretends to be desktop but acts more like iphone'), 64 on Android ('budget androids are easy to crash @ 128'), 512 desktop ('allocating 1024 often fails'); sharedWasmMemory() retries WebAssembly.Memory({shared:true}) shrinking maximum from 32767 pages until allocation succeeds; threads default = hardwareConcurrency minus 1 if odd-adjusted, fewer on mobile
Lichess caches big NNUE files client-side in OPFS/IndexedDB ('bigFileStorage') with download progress callbacks and deletes them on BAD_NNUE engine error — replicate for any optional big-net mode

GitHub Pages limits: published site ≤ 1GB, soft 100GB/month bandwidth, soft 10 builds/hr (no limit with Actions); git hard limit 100MB/file means the 113MB full SF18 wasm CANNOT be committed, but 7MB lite and even the 72.7MB big net CAN; jsDelivr npm serving has a 50MB per-file limit (sometimes 20MB for some types) so only lite flavors are CDN-servable
GPLv3 applies to both nmrugg/stockfish.js and @lichess-org/stockfish-web (LICENSE 35,149 B = GPL-3.0) — StockThink repo must be GPLv3-compatible open source (fine per project plan)

Lichess win% conversion: rawWinningChances = 2/(1+exp(-0.00368208*cp))-1 with cp clamped to ±1000; mate mapped via cp=(21-min(10,|mate|))*100; povDiff = (povChances(e1)-povChances(e2))/2 — the standard base for accuracy/move-classification math

hi-ogawa builds (hi-ogawa/Stockfish = stockfish-nnue.wasm, SF14 era) are obsolete; his SIMD/NNUE-speed work was upstreamed and he is credited in both stockfish.js and stockfish-web; lila marks __sf14nnue obsoletedBy 'dynamicImportFromWorker'
key_facts.source: https://github.com/hi-ogawa/Stockfish + unpkg stockfish README credits + lila engines.ts

research:Running Stockfish in the browse.recommendation: Default engine: vendor stockfish-18-lite-single.js + .wasm (7.3MB total, NNUE, single-thread, zero headers needed) from npm 'stockfish'@18.0.7 directly into the GitHub Pages repo (well under the 100MB git limit; jsDelivr npm/stockfish@18.0.7/bin/... works as a runtime fallback URL since the lite files are <50MB). Load it as a plain Web Worker speaking raw UCI via postMessage/onmessage. Port lichess's protocol.ts almost verbatim for the UCI layer: set UCI_AnalyseMode=true, UCI_Chess960=true, MultiPV=2 (need second-best line for Great/Miss/alternative detection; MultiPV=3 only if cheap), Hash=32 (iOS) / 64 (mobile) / 256-512 (desktop), one in-flight search per worker with stop-then-swap queueing, parse info lines with the depth/nodes/multipv/score/pv switch and remember the cp sign is side-to-move POV. Analysis budget: drive by 'go movetime' with an adaptive target — calibrate nps once at startup (short fixed-nodes search or 'bench'), then pick movetime so the whole game fits the user's chosen budget (Fast ~300ms/pos, Standard ~600ms, Deep ~1200ms); this lands at roughly 100k-700k nodes/position, bracketing lichess's autoTutor (100k) and approaching manualRequest (1M) — genuinely review-grade. Analyze positions sequentially per worker but pool 2 workers (positions are independent; ~2x wall-clock win, ~15MB extra RAM); cap at 1 worker on mobile (memory). Always recreate a worker via terminate()+new on error/timeout rather than trying to recover. Strong mode (optional, feature-flagged): ship gzuidhof/coi-serviceworker.js at site root and offer multi-threaded stockfish-18-lite (Threads = min(hardwareConcurrency-1, 4-8)) when window.crossOriginIsolated===true after the one-time reload; accept the Safari double-reload and Firefox-private failure by gating purely on the runtime crossOriginIsolated flag and silently falling back to single-thread. Skip @lichess-org/stockfish-web for v1 (all builds hard-require SharedArrayBuffer) but consider it later for a 'maximum strength' tier: sf_18_smallnet (665KB wasm + 10.4MB net) or sf_18 + 72.7MB big net committed to the repo and cached in OPFS/IndexedDB lichess-style. License: everything is GPLv3 — make the StockThink repo GPLv3. Don't bother with hi-ogawa builds (obsolete, upstreamed) or the 113MB full builds (exceed GitHub's 100MB file limit and jsDelivr's 50MB limit).

research:The zero-cost natural-language.summary:
Researched zero-cost natural-language commentary for StockThink across 6 areas, with empirical verification (curl tests against Pollinations and HuggingFace) and primary sources (WebLLM config.ts, HF API, arxiv papers).

IN-BROWSER LLMs: WebLLM (@mlc-ai/web-llm, Apache TVM, WebGPU) is the strongest option. Verified exact download sizes by summing HF repo trees: SmolLM2-360M-Instruct q4f16 207MB (376MB VRAM), Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct q4f16 290MB (945MB VRAM), Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct q4f16 705MB (879MB VRAM), Qwen2.5-1.5B q4f16 880MB (1630MB VRAM), SmolLM2-1.7B 966MB (1774MB VRAM), gemma-2-2b 1493MB (1895MB VRAM). All marked low_resource_required:true except gemma. All mlc-ai HF repos verified gated:false/private:false including the Llama-3.2 conversion — no auth, and HF echoes arbitrary Origins in access-control-allow-origin (verified), so GitHub Pages can fetch them; weights cache locally (Cache API/IndexedDB) so download is one-time. WebLLM paper (MacBook M3 Max, Chrome): Phi-3.5-mini 71.1 tok/s, Llama-3.1-8B 41.1 tok/s, ~80% of native; a 0.5–1.5B model comfortably exceeds 20 tok/s on most WebGPU laptops (15 tok/s reported on Intel Iris Xe for 1.5B). WebLLM supports OpenAI-style API with streaming, seed, and "state-of-the-art JSON mode structured generation". transformers.js v3 (ONNX Runtime Web) is the alternative; onnx-community/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct q4f16 is 483MB.

WEBGPU SUPPORT 2026 (~82.3% global, caniuse): Chrome/Edge 113+ (Android 121+, Linux partial since 144/147); Firefox 141 Windows (Jul 2025), 145/147 macOS, Linux expected 2026, Android flagged; Safari 26 across macOS/iOS/visionOS. So AI-mode must be progressive enhancement.

QUALITY: The CCC paper (arxiv 2410.20811) is the key evidence: even GPT-4o freely commenting on chess scores 0.36 correctness with 0.46 illegal-move/nonexistent-piece hallucination rate; feeding it engine-derived concepts lifts correctness to 0.60 (human reference 0.62) and halves hallucination to 0.20. Non-GPT-4o models solve <12% of chess problems. Conclusion: NO model may compute chess facts; a 0.5–2B model can only reword pre-verified facts, and outputs must be verified (whitelist check that mentioned squares/pieces/moves appear in the fact set; fall back to template on failure). IFEval (SmolLM2 paper Table 5): SmolLM2-1.7B 56.7, Llama-3.2-1B 53.5, Qwen2.5-1.5B 47.4 — i.e., 1–2B models follow constraints only ~50–60% of the time, so verification+fallback is mandatory, and viable.

TEMPLATES: E2E NLG Challenge (62 systems) found seq2seq "can be outperformed by hand-engineered systems in terms of overall quality" — template systems topped human quality ratings in a constrained domain exactly like move commentary. research:The zero-cost natural-language.recommendation: Build a 3-tier hybrid commentary engine. TIER 1 (default, always works, offline, deterministic): template/slot-filling NLG driven entirely by verified facts from Stockfish + a chess.js-based concept detector. Structure: per-classification phrase banks (8-15 variants each) x concept clauses (hangs piece, missed fork, opens file, king safety drop, eval swing) with seeded-random selection keyed on move number for reproducibility. This matches what chess.com itself apparently ships, and the E2E NLG Challenge showed hand-engineered templates beat neural systems on human-rated quality in exactly this kind of constrained domain. "Explain more" = expanding the same fact set into a longer multi-sentence template (refutation line in SAN, eval numbers, principle explanation). TIER 2 (optional "AI phrasing" toggle, WebGPU only): WebLLM with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct-q4f16_1-MLC (880MB download, 1.6GB VRAM) as default and SmolLM2-360M/Qwen2.5-0.5B (207-290MB) as a low-resource choice; feature-detect navigator.gpu and shader-f16 (fall back to q4f32 variants); load via ServiceWorkerMLCEngine with Cache API so the download is one-time. Prompt = "Rewrite these verified facts as one natural sentence. Facts: classification=Blunder; played=Qxb7; hangs=knight on f5; refutation=Bxf5; evalswing=-3.2. Mention ONLY these facts." with seed set and low temperature. CRITICALLY: never ask the model for chess analysis (CCC paper: even GPT-4o hallucinates 46% of the time unguided), and verify every output with a whitelist check (every square/piece/move token in the output must appear in the fact set; reject hedging/extra claims) — on failure regenerate once, then fall back to the Tier 1 template. Expect ~50-60% first-pass constraint compliance (IFEval) — the verifier+fallback makes this safe. TIER 3 (dev-time, optional): a repo skill where the user pastes a PGN into Claude Code (Max subscription, no API key) and it emits commentary JSON for featured/showcase games, loaded statically by the site. Do NOT build on Pollinations (verified: new endpoint requires keys, legacy endpoint 429s at 1 request/IP) or any "free API" tier; optionally note Puter.js as a future experiment only. All model downloads come keyless from the HF CDN with permissive CORS (verified) — no auth anywhere in the stack.

research:How chess.com's Game Review / m.summary:
Chess.com's Game Review classifies moves via an "Expected Points Model": win probability (0.00–1.00) derived from engine eval AND player rating (rating-adjusted since the Feb-2025 CAPS2 update). The official support article publishes the expected-points-LOST ladder: Best=0.00, Excellent 0–0.02, Good 0.02–0.05, Inaccuracy 0.05–0.10, Mistake 0.10–0.20, Blunder 0.20–1.00. So it is win-probability-loss based, not raw centipawn-loss. Special classifications sit outside the table: Brilliant = a good piece sacrifice where you are not bad afterwards and were not already completely winning; Great = best/near-best move that is "critical to the outcome" (only good move / turning point); Miss = failing to punish an opponent's mistake (Ø glyph). Accuracy is CAPS2 (trade secret): each move scored by category, "mostly averaged" with adjustments for mate-distance and reduced penalty for repeated blunders; book moves count as best; scores deliberately redistributed to a 50–95 "academic grading" feel; engine depth varies with player strength.

Open-source reverse-engineerings, all fetched verbatim from raw source: (1) WintrCat/freechess (the classic "Game Report", CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 — NOT code-license-friendly): pure-centipawn quadratic thresholds via getEvaluationLossThreshold(), mate-transition decision tables, Brilliant via hand-rolled isPieceHanging/getAttackers/getDefenders sacrifice detection (non-pawn non-king piece left hanging, capture not refuted by pins/mate-in-1, not winning anyway i.e. 2nd line < +700, eval ≥ 0, no promotion, not in check), Great = previous move was Blunder + top-vs-2nd-line gap ≥ 150cp + moved piece not hanging, accuracy = mean of per-classification values (blunder 0 … best 1) ×100. No Miss. (2) WintrCat/wintrchess (successor, GPL-3.0): expected points = 1/(1+e^(−0.0035·cp)); point-loss ladder Best<0.01, Excellent<0.045, Okay<0.08, Inaccuracy<0.12, Mistake<0.22, else Blunder; "Critical" (=Great) requires top move played + 2nd-best move would lose ≥0.10 expected points + not free-material capture + not having mate; Brilliant via unsafe/trapped-piece analysis; per-move accuracy 103.16·e^(−4·pointLoss)−3.17, game accuracy = plain mean per side. (3) GuillaumeSD/Chesskit (chesskit.org, AGPL-3.0): Lichess Win% based; ladder on win% diff: <−20 Blunder, <−10 Mistake, <−5 Inaccuracy, <−2 Okay, else Excellent; "Splendid"(Brilliant) = sacrifice + winDiff ≥ −2 + not losing + alternative line not >97% winning; "Perfect"(Great) = winDiff ≥ −2 + (outcome flipped across 50% with >10 gain OR only good move: ≥10% better than 2nd line); estimated Elo from ACPL: 3100·e^(−0.01·acpl), anchored to known rating via rating·e^(−0.005·(gameCpl−expectedCpl)).

Lichess (verified in scalachess eval.scala and lila AccuracyPercent.scala/Advice.scala): winningChances = 2/(1+exp(−0.00368208·cp))−1 with cp clamped ±1000 (so the candidate fact Win% = 50+50·(2/(1+exp(−0.00368208·cp))−1) is CONFIRMED); judgements on winning-chances delta ([−1,1] scale): ≥0.3 Blunder, ≥0.2 Mistake, ≥0.1 Inaccuracy (i.e. 15/10/5 Win%-points); move Accuracy% = 103.1668100711649·exp(−0.04354415386753951·winDiff)−3.166924740191411, plus +1 "uncertainty bonus", clamped [0,100] (candidate formula CONFIRMED); game accuracy = mean of (volatility-weighted mean, harmonic mean), window = clamp(nMoves/10, 2, 8), weight = stdev of window Win% clamped [0.5, 12]. Mate advice: losing a forced mate or allowing one is Blunder unless already ±700cp (Mistake) or ±999cp (Inaccuracy); slower mate = no judgement.

key_facts.claim: chess.com classification is win-probability based ('Expected Points', rating-dependent). Official expected-points-LOST thresholds: Best 0.00, Excellent 0.00–0.02, Good 0.02–0.05, Inaccuracy 0.05–0.10, Mistake 0.10–0.20, Blunder 0.20–1.00

chess.com Brilliant = good piece sacrifice + not in a bad position after the move + not already completely winning; Great = best/near-best move critical to game outcome; Miss = failed to capitalize on opponent's mistake; all rating-adjusted

CAPS2 (chess.com accuracy) is a trade secret: per-move category scores 'mostly averaged', adjusted for mate-distance and reduced multi-blunder penalty; book moves scored as best; engine depth varies by player strength; scores redistributed so most players land 50–95

freechess classification logic lives in src/lib/classification.ts + src/lib/analysis.ts + src/lib/board.ts; thresholds are quadratic functions of |prevEval| in centipawns (e.g. Excellent threshold = 0.0002·prevEval² + 0.1231·prevEval + 27.5455)

freechess Great move = previous position classified BLUNDER + |topEval − secondTopEval| ≥ 150cp + destination piece not hanging + no mate involved; Brilliant requires eval ≥ 0 for mover, 2nd-best line < +700cp, no promotion, non-pawn piece left capturable ('hanging') whose capture isn't refuted by pin or (for <rook value) mate-in-1

freechess accuracy = arithmetic mean of per-move classification values ×100 (blunder 0, mistake 0.2, inaccuracy 0.4, good 0.65, excellent 0.9, best/great/brilliant/book/forced 1) — NOT chess.com's method
freechess LICENSE is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (NonCommercial-ShareAlike) — verbatim code reuse forces NC-SA terms; do not copy code, only re-implement the (uncopyrightable) thresholds/algorithm ideas

wintrchess (successor, GPL-3.0) switched to expected points = 1/(1+exp(−0.0035·cp)); point-loss ladder: <0.01 Best, <0.045 Excellent, <0.08 Okay, <0.12 Inaccuracy, <0.22 Mistake, else Blunder (shared/src/lib/reporter/classification/pointLoss.ts)

wintrchess 'Critical' (its Great equivalent) = top move played + expected-points loss of 2nd-best line ≥ 0.1 + not a capture of free material + you don't have forced mate; move accuracy = 103.16·exp(−4·pointLoss) − 3.17 (lichess curve rescaled to 0–1 expected points), game accuracy = plain per-side mean

Lichess Win% = 50 + 50·winningChances, winningChances = 2/(1+exp(−0.00368208·cp)) − 1, with cp clamped to ±1000 and mate mapped to ±1000cp — candidate fact CONFIRMED verbatim in scalachess core/src/main/scala/eval.scala

Lichess Inaccuracy/Mistake/Blunder = winning-chances delta ≥ 0.1 / 0.2 / 0.3 on the [−1,1] scale (= 5/10/15 Win% points); mate-related: allowing or losing forced mate = Blunder, downgraded to Mistake if already past ±700cp and Inaccuracy past ±999cp; a slower mate gets no judgement (lila modules/tree/src/main/Advice.scala)

Lichess move Accuracy% = 103.1668100711649·exp(−0.04354415386753951·winDiff) − 3.166924740191411 + 1 (uncertainty bonus), clamped [0,100]; winDiff in Win% points; 100 if position improved — candidate formula CONFIRMED (lila modules/analyse/src/main/AccuracyPercent.scala)
Lichess game accuracy = (volatility-weighted mean + harmonic mean)/2 of per-move accuracies; sliding window size = clamp(numMoves/10, 2, 8); weight = stdev of Win% in window clamped to [0.5, 12]

Chesskit (chesskit.org, AGPL-3.0) is the cleanest client-side TS reference: ladder on Win% diff (<−20 Blunder, <−10 Mistake, <−5 Inaccuracy, <−2 Okay, else Excellent); 'Splendid'=Brilliant needs sacrifice + winDiff ≥ −2 + not losing + alternative line ≤ 97% winning; 'Perfect'=Great needs winDiff ≥ −2 + (outcome flip across 50% with >10 gain OR ≥10% better than 2nd-best line)

Estimated game Elo in Chesskit: eloFromAcpl = 3100·exp(−0.01·acpl); if player rating known: estElo = rating·exp(−0.005·(gameCpl − expectedCplForRating)) where expectedCpl = −100·ln(min(rating,3100)/3100); ACPL per move capped at 1000

No major open-source clone implements chess.com's 'Miss' (freechess/wintrchess/Chesskit all lack it); chess.com defines it textually as 'fail to capitalize on your opponent's mistake' (Ø glyph) — must be specced from scratch: opponent's prior move was Mistake/Blunder/Missed-win AND your reply forfeits the winning expected-points swing

freechess applies softening rules: a Blunder is downgraded to Good if mover is still ≥ +600cp after the move, or was already ≤ −600cp before it; first moves matching its openings.json book (or cloud-eval positions) are reclassified as Book

Licensing: lila & Chesskit are AGPL-3.0, wintrchess GPL-3.0, freechess CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Formulas/constants are facts (not copyrightable) and can be re-implemented in an MIT/Apache StockThink, but copying code verbatim triggers copyleft/NC obligations

research:How chess.com's Game Review / m.recommendation: Build StockThink's classifier on the win-probability stack, not raw centipawns: (1) convert Stockfish cp to Win% with the verified Lichess formula (clamp cp to ±1000, mate = ±1000cp; for terminal mate use 100/0 like Chesskit); (2) classify with chess.com's OFFICIAL expected-points-lost table (Best 0, Excellent ≤0.02, Good ≤0.05, Inaccuracy ≤0.10, Mistake ≤0.20, Blunder >0.20 — divide Win% diff by 100 to get expected points), giving an authentic chess.com feel with publicly documented thresholds; (3) Book = position FEN found in an openings.json (bundle the same Lichess openings TSV freechess/Chesskit use); Forced = only one legal move; checkmate delivered = Best. For Brilliant, re-implement (clean-room) the freechess algorithm — it is the best-documented heuristic: top/near-top move, eval ≥ 0 for mover, second-best line < +700cp ("not winning anyway"), no promotion, not escaping check, leaves a non-pawn piece "hanging" per the isPieceHanging/attacker-defender rules, with the pin/mate-in-1 refutation pass — but DO NOT copy freechess code (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 is incompatible with a normal code license); Chesskit's simpler sacrifice test (AGPL) is an alternative if StockThink itself goes (A)GPL-3.0. For Great, combine Chesskit's "Perfect" rules (winDiff ≥ −2 AND (outcome flips across 50% with >10 gain OR move is ≥10 Win% better than the 2nd engine line)) — this requires MultiPV=2 from stockfish.js, which you need anyway. Implement Miss yourself (no clone has it): if the opponent's previous move lost ≥0.10 expected points (their Mistake/Blunder) and your reply gives back most of that swing (e.g., your move loses ≥0.05 expected points relative to the punishing line, or drops you from >65% back to ≤55%), tag it Miss instead of Inaccuracy/Mistake. For accuracy, use the exact published Lichess formula (per-move 103.1668·e^(−0.04354·winDiff)−3.1669, game = mean of volatility-weighted mean (window clamp(n/10,2,8), weight stdev clamped [0.5,12]) and harmonic mean) — it's an official, documented, well-behaved metric; do not chase the secret CAPS2. For estimated game Elo, use Chesskit's ACPL model: 3100·e^(−0.01·acpl), anchored to a user-supplied rating via rating·e^(−0.005·(gameCpl−expectedCpl)) — note it comes from an AGPL repo, so re-derive from the cited lichess forum source or keep the two-line formula as an uncopyrightable fact. License StockThink MIT/GPL accordingly and credit freechess/wintrchess/Chesskit/lila as research references, never as copied code unless adopting GPL/AGPL.

corrections: CAPS2 claim has WRONG SOURCE ATTRIBUTION and overstated paraphrase: support article 8708970 (fetched twice) contains ONLY: CAPS2 name ('an improved version of the original Chess.com CAPS'), 'Moves are still compared against the top engine recommendations, but the math on how these are calculated has changed', the school-test analogy, and 'the majority of scores now fall mostly between 50 and 95'. It does NOT contain 'mostly averaged', mate-distance, multi-blunder, book-move, or engine-depth statements, nor the words 'trade secret'. Those quotes are real chess.com STAFF statements but come from an emailed 'Reply from Chess.com' quoted in https://saychess.substack.com/p/what-chess-players-need-to-know-about: 'We then mostly average everything to see how accurately you played overall', 'we smooth out the edges by adjusting for things like mate-distance and reducing the penalty for multiple blunders', 'Book moves are always scored as best', 'The depth we use varies based on player strength and settings'. Treat as secondary-sourced staff statements, not help-center text.

chess.com 'Great = best/near-best move critical to game outcome' is IMPRECISE: the 'always the best or nearly best move' sentence in article 8572705 describes BRILLIANT, not Great. Great verbatim: 'These are moves that were critical to the outcome of the game, such as turning a losing position into an equal one, an equal position into a winning one, or finding the only good move in a position. Similar to Brilliant Moves, we are more generous in what we call a Great Move for new players...'. The researcher omitted the 'finding the only good move' criterion — which is exactly what wintrchess Critical and Chesskit Perfect implement (source: https://support.chess.com/en/articles/8572705).

wintrchess source path error: the move-accuracy formula 103.16·exp(−4·pointLoss)−3.17 and the per-colour plain mean live in shared/src/lib/reporter/accuracy.ts, NOT in classification/critical.ts (critical.ts holds only the Critical logic). Verified via GitHub contents API listing of shared/src/lib/reporter (files: accuracy.ts, classify.ts, expectedPoints.ts, report.ts; classification/ = brilliant.ts, critical.ts, pointLoss.ts).

wintrchess pointLoss claim is INCOMPLETE: pointLoss.ts also contains mate-transition ladders the claim omitted — mate→mate: BEST if mateLoss<0 (or ==0 while losing), EXCELLENT if mateLoss<2, OKAY if <7, else INACCURACY; mate→cp (lost a forced mate): EXCELLENT if subjective cp ≥800, OKAY ≥400, INACCURACY ≥200, MISTAKE ≥0, else BLUNDER; cp→mate (allowed mate, values are mate distances): BEST if >0, BLUNDER if ≥−2, MISTAKE if ≥−5, else INACCURACY (source: raw wintrchess pointLoss.ts).

Chesskit Perfect ('Great') sharpened against verbatim code: 'only good move' is STRICT > 10 not ≥ 10: getIsTheOnlyGoodMove returns (positionWin% − lastPositionAlternativeLineWin%)·(isWhiteMove?1:−1) > 10; hasChangedGameOutcome requires BOTH winPercentageDiff > 10 AND crossing 50 ((last<50 && pos>50) || (last>50 && pos<50)); plus three guards the claim partly omitted: return false if winPercentageDiff < −2, if isSimplePieceRecapture(fenTwoMovesAgo, uciMoves), or if isLosingOrAlternateCompletelyWinning (mover losing OR alternative line >97% for white / <3% for black — same guard as Splendid) (source: raw Chesskit moveClassification.ts).

verify:chesscom-review.additions: CRITICAL DEPLOYMENT FACTS MISSED (engine on GitHub Pages): (1) GitHub Pages serves static files and provides no way to set custom HTTP response headers, so COOP/COEP cross-origin isolation cannot be enabled server-side; without it SharedArrayBuffer is unavailable and MULTI-THREADED Stockfish WASM will not run. Workaround verified: gzuidhof/coi-serviceworker (MIT license) — 'Cross-origin isolation (COOP and COEP) through a service worker for situations in which you can't control the headers (e.g. GH pages)'; caveats: it force-reloads the page on first visit, and must be served as a separate same-origin file (not bundled, not from a CDN). (2) Engine builds (nmrugg/stockfish.js README, currently Stockfish 18, GPLv3): full NNUE build >100MB (poor UX, near GitHub's 100MB-per-file git limit — would need chunking or external CDN); 'lite' NNUE build ~7MB; each available single-threaded (no special headers needed, ignores 'setoption name Threads') and multi-threaded (requires COOP/COEP/SharedArrayBuffer); README recommends lite single-threaded for most projects — the right default for StockThink, with optional multi-threaded upgrade via coi-serviceworker. Engine GPLv3 is compatible with an MIT app if the engine ships as a separate worker file (mere aggregation) with license attribution. (3) GitHub Pages limits (docs.github.com): published site ≤ 1GB, soft 100GB/month bandwidth, soft 10 builds/hour (waived when deploying via custom Actions workflow) — another reason to avoid the >100MB engine: ~1300 visitor loads/month would exhaust bandwidth. SPEC FACTS MISSED: (4) wintrchess Brilliant logic (shared/src/lib/reporter/classification/brilliant.ts) was never specced: requires the move to be a critical-candidate (top move), promotions excluded ('Promotions cannot be brilliant'), must leave ≥1 unsafe piece (unsafePieces.length > 0), and is denied if the move reduces the number of unsafe pieces in non-check positions, if unsafe pieces are adequately covered by counterthreats/danger-level defenses, if all unsafe pieces are trapped anyway, if the moved piece was itself trapped, or if the trapped-piece count decreases — i.e. sacrificing trapped/doomed material never counts. Useful as a second reference implementation alongside freechess's isPieceHanging+refutation approach. (5) freechess board.ts pieceValues confirmed verbatim {p:1,n:3,b:3,r:5,q:9,k:Infinity,m:0} with isPieceHanging rules: not hanging after an equal-or-better trade; rook-takes-minor-defended-by-one-minor counts as favorable (not hanging); hanging if any attacker is worth less than the piece; if attackers > defenders, hanging unless capture itself is a sacrifice with a cheaper defender present; pawn among defenders ⇒ not hanging. (6) chess.com's 'Great' includes 'finding the only good move in a position' — this maps directly onto wintrchess Critical (secondTopMovePointLoss ≥ 0.1) and Chesskit getIsTheOnlyGoodMove (>10 Win% over alternative), giving a sound spec for StockThink's Great. (7) For a Miss spec, chess.com's verbatim definition adds the outcome clause the claim truncated: '...miss the opportunity to gain a winning position, often resulting in an equal or worse outcome' — i.e. Miss should require: opponent's previous move lost significant expected points (their Mistake/Blunder), your best reply reached a winning band, and your actual reply fell back to equal-or-worse; no open-source reference exists, so it must be parameterized in StockThink's own expected-points terms. (8) The support-article table covers only the six point-loss bands; Brilliant/Great/Miss (and Book/Forced, which the article doesn't tabulate) are rule-based overlays — matching how all three clones structure their code (ladder first, then special-move overrides).
